stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 6th.
stocks.news:
Wall Street bounced back Monday as Iran ceasefire hopes gave bulls something to buy. Tech led the charge — the Nasdaq gained over half a percent.

Micron is living every chipmaker’s dream — sold out for the entire year. Its memory chips power AI servers, and with the Big Five planning $600B+ in AI spending this year, Micron’s order book is a who’s-who of Silicon Valley.

On the lighter side, Trump’s new investment accounts for kids are getting real — BNY and Robinhood are building the app. Every eligible child born 2025–2028 gets a $1,000 Treasury deposit. Over 4 million kids already signed up. Starting them young.

Airlines are white-knuckling fuel costs — United burned $400M more than expected last month and hiked bag fees $10 to cushion the blow. Delta reports this week. Don’t expect calm skies.

economy.commentary:
The clock is ticking. Trump gave Iran until Tuesday 8 PM to reopen the Strait of Hormuz — or face escalation. Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey pushed for a 45-day ceasefire, but as of this morning, Iran rejected the deal.

JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon isn’t sugar-coating it — he says the Iran war could wreck 2026’s economy. Oil crossed $100 after the strait closed, and he believes it’s erasing gains from AI productivity and the big tax cuts. Goldman Sachs raised recession odds to 30%.

Gold is steady near $4,685, barely flinching despite the geopolitical drama. The 10-year yield held flat at 4.33% — bond markets in wait-and-see mode ahead of tonight’s deadline.

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court ended Trump’s original tariffs in February, but Section 301 could bring them back — covering 16 trading partners and roughly 60–70% of U.S. imports. Another domino to watch.

crypto.commentary:
Crypto caught a tailwind from ceasefire rumors — BTC rallied to $69,957 and ETH pushed above $2,065. The combined value of all altcoins briefly crossed $1.1 trillion, a milestone that felt distant six months ago when Bitcoin was sliding from its $126K peak.

Bitmine is quietly building an ETH empire — now the largest Ethereum holder on Earth with 4.8 million ETH worth $10.2B. That stash generates an estimated $282M/year from staking alone. Coinbase sits in second with about $8B worth.

The institutional wave keeps building. Schwab, Morgan Stanley, and Coinbase are all expanding crypto access. When firms managing trillions put crypto on the same menu as stocks and bonds, the floor keeps getting higher.

real_estate.homebuilder: The Iran deadline expires tonight — and mortgage rates are treading water near 6.43% as oil keeps the Fed sidelined heading into peak spring buying season.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 16-city tracker slipped 6 units to 2,495. But new listings climbed to 182 (from 176), and coming-soon rose to 272 (from 261). Sellers are gearing up even as total supply edges lower.

📍 Fremont: Holding steady at 297 (up 1). 28 new listings this week and 58 coming soon — spring sellers are active.

📍 Union City: Down 1 to 81. Still one of the tightest markets in our tracker.

📍 Oakland: Down 4 to 728 with 41 new listings. Buyers continue absorbing inventory faster than it’s listed.

📍 Milpitas: Down 1 to 115 — just 1 new listing across the entire city. Inventory shift is coming from pending conversions, not new sellers.

📍 Newark: Slipped 2 to 99, dipping below the century mark.

📍 Dublin: Down sharply to 82 with 27 new listings. A significant batch of pendings likely closed or expired this week.

Nationally, median home prices hit $398,000 in February — the highest ever for the month. The Mortgage Bankers Association says rates stay above 6% all of 2026. Fannie Mae says they could drop to 5.7% by December — but that forecast came before the Iran war pushed oil higher. Over 30 years, even that small gap means tens of thousands in extra interest.
